one_line_takeaway: A 48-page survey that recasts LLM-agent security, privacy, and ethics threats under a source-by-type taxonomy and a six-feature agent model, mapping the attack/defense literature and its gaps rather than running experiments.
key_finding: The paper's main 'finding' is organizational: a source/type binary taxonomy resolves the cross-module and cross-stage mislabeling problem of prior agent-security taxonomies, and mapping threats onto six agent-specific features exposes where research is thin. Concrete gaps it surfaces from the surveyed literature include: adversarial-example work on agents is stuck on untargeted attacks and confined to the output module, ignoring memory/tool/planner attack surfaces and joint robustness across the multiple LLMs inside one agent; model-extraction research barely addresses that an attacker's observed input/output differs from the internal LLM's (e.g. WebGPT, HuggingGPT), yet stealing a shared commercial controller (ChatGPT used by Voyager, PReP, ChatDev) could compromise all agents built on it; prompt-leakage defenses ignore internal components like the Planner; hallucination lacks a mathematical definition, multimodal (speech/video) benchmarks, and specialized-domain evaluation, and RAG can inject new 'imitation falsehood' hallucinations. It repeats cited evidence that agent risks are real (INJECAGENT: ReAct-prompted GPT-4 vulnerable to indirect prompt injection in nearly a quarter of tested cases; low-resource languages ~3x more likely to elicit harmful jailbreak content).
core_contribution: A survey (Gan et al., Zhejiang University et al.) that organizes the security, privacy, and ethics threats facing LLM-based agents. Its central original contribution is a NOVEL taxonomy that abandons the module-based and stage-based schemes of the two prior surveys it explicitly contrasts against (Cui et al. and Deng et al.) and instead maps each threat into a binary table by (a) SOURCE and (b) TYPE. Sources are: problematic inputs (attacker crafts inputs but cannot touch the model, e.g. adversarial examples, prompt injection), model flaws (benign inputs but the model's own defects cause bad behavior, e.g. hallucination, bias, memorization), and combined threats (crafted inputs exploiting model defects, e.g. backdoors, privacy leakage). Types are: security/safety (incorrect outputs), privacy (leakage), and ethics (unfairness/bias). The authors argue this source/type framing pinpoints cross-module and cross-stage threats (e.g. privacy leakage that originates in the LM's memory but surfaces at the output module) that the module/stage taxonomies mislabel. Two further self-claimed contributions: (2) explicit coverage of multi-modal LLMs (MLLMs), which prior text-only surveys neglected, and (3) four case studies of real deployed agents to show that actual agents omit modules and face scenario-specific risks. The survey also defines a general four-module agent framework (Input Module, Decision Module, External Entities, Output Module) and identifies six key features that create new attack surfaces relative to traditional DNNs/RL agents: LLM-based controller, multi-modal I/O, multi-source inputs, multi-round interaction, memory mechanism, and tool invocation, then maps every threat class onto these features.
threat_model: As a survey, it does not propose a single threat model but catalogs a landscape. Adversary capability is stratified by SOURCE: (1) input-only adversaries who cannot modify model weights but craft inputs (adversarial examples, goal hijacking / direct and indirect prompt injection, prompt leaking and prompt stealing, jailbreaking, model extraction via queries); (2) no-adversary 'model flaw' risks where benign use still yields harm (hallucination, training-data memorization, bias/fairness); (3) combined adversaries who plant model defects and trigger them (backdoors that persist through safety training, privacy leakage extracting training or contextual data). Agent-specific escalations emphasized: indirect prompt injection through tool/API and RAG-database contents, infectious jailbreaks that propagate across cooperating multi-agent systems via shared memory, goal hijacking of the Planner sub-component, and prompt leakage of internal (non-user-facing) Planner prompts. Attackers may exploit any of the six features (e.g. hiding malicious prompts across modalities, forging chat logs across multi-round context, poisoning memory/RAG).
limitations: (1) It is a descriptive/organizational survey with no experiments, no reproduction, and no quantitative evaluation of the attacks or defenses it catalogs, so relative severity of threats is not measured. (2) The taxonomy's boundaries are partly definitional (e.g. classifying a threat by whether the output is 'incorrect' vs merely 'unfair') and could be contested for borderline threats. (3) Fast-moving field: an arXiv v1 dated Nov 2024, so coverage is a snapshot. (5) Many of the strongest-sounding numeric claims (e.g. the 'quarter of cases' and '3x' figures) are drawn from cited primary works, not measured by this survey.

datasets_used: No datasets are used by the survey itself; it catalogs benchmarks from the literature (Fig. 9). Goal-hijacking/prompt-injection: INJECAGENT, InjectBench, BIPIA
Jailbreaking: JailBreakV-28K, MM-SafetyBench, RED-EVAL, JailbreakBench, AdvBench, JailbreakEval, AttackEval, h4rm3l
Hallucination: HaluEval, HalluDial, DiaHalu, VisDiaHalBench, AMBER, Reefknot, AUTOHALLUSION, THRONE, HypoTermQA, UHGEval, HalOmi, ToolBeHonest
Bias/fairness: StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs, WinoQueer, BBQ, Grep-BiasIR, TrustGPT, RedditBias, VisoGender, VLStereoSet, SocialCounterfactuals
quantitative_results: Survey scope: ~48 pages; 3 threat types (security/safety, privacy, ethics) x 3 threat sources (problematic inputs, model flaws, combined) taxonomy; 6 key agent features; 4-module agent framework; 4 agent case studies
References run past [337] (in-text markers include [259], [325], [327], [337]), i.e. 300+ references; exact count not verified from the extracted pages
Cited (not the survey's own): INJECAGENT reports ReAct-prompted GPT-4 vulnerable to indirect prompt injection in nearly 25% of tested cases
Cited (not the survey's own): low-resource languages ~3x more likely to elicit harmful content in jailbreak attacks (Deng et al.)

The survey produces no experimental result tables of its own; its quantitative artifacts are structured mappings (Table 1 paper-to-threat mapping, Fig. 4 feature-by-threat matrix, Fig. 9 benchmark catalog)
baselines_compared: Prior module-based agent-security survey: Cui et al. (input / LM / toolchain / output modules)
Prior stage-based agent-security survey: Deng et al. (perception / internal execution / action-in-environment / interaction-with-untrusted-entities stages)
The paper positions its source+type taxonomy against these two as more accurate for cross-module and cross-stage threats
